source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.1-15.2 review of permissions (pp. 283-285)

questions.q: In 'ls -l' output, the block of 10 characters splits how?
options: 1 file-type character, then three groups of rwx for owner, group and others
10 permission bits with no type character
5 for the owner and 5 for the group
1 type, then owner and others only
questions.why: The first character is the file type; the next nine are owner, group and others permissions (3x rwx).

questions.q: For a FILE, what does the w (write) permission allow?
options: Modifying the file's content (e.g. with vim, >>)
questions.why: On a file, w permits changing its contents; r reads it, x executes it.

questions.q: For a DIRECTORY, what does the w permission allow?
options: Creating, deleting and renaming files within it (mkdir, touch, rm)
Nothing - w is meaningless on directories
questions.why: On a directory, w lets you create/delete/rename entries; r lists the contents and x lets you cd in.

questions.q: In the numeric notation, what values do r, w and x carry?
questions.why: read=4, write=2, execute=1, summed per class (e.g. rwx = 7).

questions.q: Which file-type character represents a symbolic (soft) link?
questions.why: l is a link; d directory, b block device, c character device, s socket, p pipe, - ordinary file.
